feedback.text: I think it's very important to keep unfair commercial practices in check, especially when related to dark patterns. Aswell as the other problematic practices stated in this initiative. However, I would like to request if you could review or look into the fact that many coorporations or better said Game Publishers shut down games that consumers bought via a digital license or physical copy. Does this not go against our rights of owning a product that we paid for? Please consider adding protecting consumers to help prevent consumers lose access to products they've bought, in this case, their preferred games. In the gaming communities there's a lot of fans that do try to preserve games that Publishers or Developers have shut down or ended support for. But there's a great many more games that are still at risk or are already 'gone', without any security of support in sight. One great example of a game that I absolutely loved to play was Wildstar. A MMORPG that was online only and offered a versatile interactable world where you could interact with friends from allover the world. I had friends via this game, but due to the game being shut down in 2018 by Carbine Studios (Developers) and published by NCSoft (Publisher), I no longer can interact with now long gone friends. Few of whom, I would have loved to stay in touch with. For many gamers, games aren't just products, but also part of the gamer's their life. They forge friendships via those games and have fond memories of said games. This is also why I support the "Stop Killing Games" initiative that has currently over 1.4 million signatures. They aim to tackle just one thing. Find a way for EU law to protect consumers to at least have Publishers and/or developers design an end of support plan, that allows for offline play or fans to keep the services running for themselves and friends or the gaming community. Multiple high ranking employees or even CEO's of Publishers have count after count, given gamers worries about the future of Gaming and their beloved games or communities they might lose or already have lost. Statements such as; "gamers should get used to not owning games" is extremely worrying and I think it would be a good case, to add another part to the Digital Fairness Act. To have publishers or developers at least create new end of support plans so that games can be preserved or supported and continued unofficially by the Gaming Community and hold them accountable when they do not leave a game in any form of playable state post support. Recently a great many games had their official services shut down (hosting/servers), their support ended or are at risk of being ended, without an end of support plan and thus leaving the game in an unplayable state despite customers having paid for these products. To name a few examples that come to my mind: AdventureQuest, Battlefield 1943, Battlefield 2042, Call of Duty 4: Modern Warfare, Call of Duty: Black Ops 4 (will be unplayable when servers shut down as it doesn't have a singleplayer option). Might & Magic Heroes Online / Might & Magic Heroes VI (online content is no longer playable). Plants vs Zombies: Garden Warfare (multiplayer), Ragnarok Online, Titanfall, (Tom Clancy's) XDefiant, The Lord of the Rings: The Battle for Middle-Earth (I and II) and the aforementioned Wildstar game. Games are an extremely versatile digital medium that offers unique insights, strategy, development skills and can be a platform for people learning how to interact with one another, learn languages, meet people online, make friends and combat solitude and loneliness. Some even take in knowledge about the real world or learn about History. Losing a game, can mean a lot to a great many people within EU. Please consider looking into this issue of publishers or developers shutting down games despite consumers having bought them.
